data.title: NGL Energy Partners: Fantastic Business Continuing To Scale Very Well
data.description:
Long Time Since Last Time

It’s been a little too long since I covered NGL Energy Partners (NGL) I have to admit. When I wrote about the MLP back in November of last year, the stock was trading around the $10 mark, and has since managed to provide an excellent return, beating the S&P 500 by a pretty wide margin.

Part of my argument in the first article about why NGL was such a solid play then and still is relates to the minimum volume commitments it has.

The revenues in this segment are also heavily contracted with an average span of 9 years. This creates long-term reliable revenue generation and visibility for the company.

I argued that because of this, NGL was heavily protected from any downside to oil prices. Well, the case has shifted a little bit since then. As most know, the forecast going into 2026 was that oil would average around $50 per barrel. That has not played out, or maybe just for a week or two at the start. At the time of writing, WTI is bouncing between $90 and $100 per barrel. The current forecasts peg oil at $96 on average in 2026 and $76 in 2027. So what the original thesis talked about, namely the core commitments, is still very much part of the core. What the spike in prices has done instead is drastically increase the earnings that NGL generates. This is right now seemingly going towards buybacks and cash distributions.

Once again, NGL has proven to be a strong contender as a long-term compounder. The change in energy markets has only benefitted NGL, and I suspect there is more upside to be had. Reiterating my Strong Buy.

The Macro Change

What often happens when prices surge for commodities like oil and gas is that exploration and production companies will be drilling at much higher rates to target the increased gap between cost per barrel and net selling price per barrel. The positive consequence of this is an increased wastewater production, and a higher demand to have this handled as well.

With NGL, the company is no longer the same it was a few years ago. It has been selling off part of its legacy business revolving around NGL operations, which, compared to the Water Solutions segments, produced vastly lower EBITDA margins.

Recall that over 1.5 million barrels per day of our water disposal volume is under MVC or CVC, which allows us to get paid on volumes even if they are not disposed of.

Looking purely at the revenues, Water Solutions is not at the top, but rather sits at the bottom based on the last 10-Q results. But it’s bringing in vastly higher EBITDA because of the nature of the business model. Even in a worst-case scenario where oil demand would decline and, in turn, demand for this segment, the minimum volume commitments help shore up a large amount of revenue for the business.

But because of the high demand for these services as a means to not choke out US oil production, NGL should continue to benefit greatly over the next few years. Exxon Mobil (XOM), for example, which has a strong hold on the Delaware Basin, reported a 60% increase in production here for their operations.

Capacity limits are already present in key areas. E&P operators, midstream providers, and regulators are all grappling with how to ensure produced water takeaway doesn’t become the bottleneck for Permian oil and gas development

Annualized, the Water Solutions segment is pulling in about $616 million in EBITDA per year based on the last quarter's figures (see image above). For a company with a market cap of just $1.78 billion, this puts the units at a still very attractive valuation, even if they’ve appreciated over 350% in the past 12 months.

In these past few weeks I’ve grappled with the thought that forecasts put oil prices going lower next year, by a moderate bit as well. Falling from $96 to $78 per barrel would sort of imply a market that is showing less demand. But I can’t see this scenario really playing out when you also consider the fact that countries' oil reserves are depleting every day as long as this conflict in the Middle East continues. That reserve will have to be replenished. So my thought then goes to that the drop in oil won’t really play out because demand will be there to push it up. Rebuilding reserves would go beyond the everyday usage of oil, adding that premium to the WTI price. How this all relates to NGL is that companies would still continue to drill at a high rate to capture these price levels. As we know, more oil production means more revenues and earnings for NGL.

The US has seen a sharp decline in oil reserves from the highs it had between 2005 and 2020. But the region that would be filling this void the most seems to be countries in Asia, which have a much more direct exposure to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. This would directly help raise the price of WTI, which indirectly benefits NGL as well.

Valued On A Legacy Business No Longer There

I mentioned earlier that NGL is set to report over $600 million in EBITDA from its water solutions segment, which would alone put the company at an FWD EV/EBITDA of just 9.3. What I see could potentially play out here is that NGL starts to aggressively lower their debt levels, which in theory could unlock much more market value assuming that the enterprise value stays the same. The idea being that $500 million in debt repayments, for instance, would need to result in $500 million more in market cap to keep the EV at the same level.

The majority of the debt is found in senior notes maturing in 2029 and 2032 at 8.125% and 8.375% rates. This is relatively high, and NGL is paying a pretty big price tag for this right now. Last quarter's interest expenses were $63 million. Based on the $2.962 billion debt position and $252 million in annualized interest expenses, NGL has a cost of debt of 8.5%. There’s no way of sugarcoating this; the cost is very high.

The idea here is that NGL is now generating a strong amount of free cash flow that will be sufficient to pay down debts at a moderate pace. Right now asset sales are a big reason for the increase here, like the $161.2 million in proceeds reported in the last 9 months. So the now core earnings segment, Water Solutions, is paying for the CapEx buildout, and the asset sales are helping pay down debt and buy back preferred shares to bring distributions lower and into a more manageable state.

Preferred shares are, for NGL right now, a very expensive debt type, sort of. Because of the mandatory distributions, it does put a strain on the cash flow statement, but the aggressive buybacks have certainly helped to ease this.

We are still guiding our full year EBITDA to a range of $650 to $660 million. These new contracted volumes that have recently come online set us up for a strong start to fiscal 2027, where we are still projecting to exceed $700 million of EBITDA for the first time in the history of the partnership.

Guidance continues to look solid for the company as well, with $650 million in EBITDA guided for this year, in line with what I’ve mentioned before when adding in the other 2 segments as well. I do suspect the CapEx will be growing more moderately the next few years as the initial investment phase starts to ease off a little. This would again open up more free cash flows, raising the ceiling for how much debt repayments NGL could do. Even at $100 million, that is still about 3.3% of all LT debt. But going back to my earlier point about how this could unlock more market value, $100 million represents 5.6% of the market cap.

Then think about the fact that NGL would trade at 8.02x 2027 EV/EBITDA, which, to be fair, is above where the sector trades on average. But then look at the margins. The core business, which is now becoming Water Solutions, has a 74% EBITDA margin (last quarter), which is massively higher than the sector median of 34.62%. The other 2 segments do bring the overall EBITDA margin down to 19.02% TTM. My point here is that NGL is valued based mostly on what its legacy businesses are bringing in and not on the “new” water handling operations, which are growing rapidly and proving to also be a massive cash machine. It’s deserving of a premium rating, and I think an EV/EBITDA of 10-11 seems fitting given how resilient this segment is in terms of earnings, much due to the minimum volume commitment contracts NGL has set in place.

The Full Picture

For me, NGL Energy Partners remains a Strong Buy as the core business continues to perform very well. There are plenty of tailwinds for the Water Solutions segment, which now is the main area where NGL is generating its earnings. With some strategic asset sales, it’s also helping buy back preferred shares to ease the strain on the cash flow statement and free up cash. Cash, which I think can go towards more aggressive debt repayments, potentially helping to unlock more market cap value, seeing as debt is about 1.5x of it right now. It’s not rare for me to average up my cost basis, especially when it’s with a company that has such strong fundamentals as NGL. This only makes it easier to reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: 'Strong Buy' As 2026 Milestones Could Bolster Its Prospects
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Bristol-Myers Squibb: Solid Tumor Collaboration With Janux And Camzyos Expansion." With respect to this article, I mentioned that it was in the process of working with Janux Therapeutics (JANX) to develop T-cell engager candidates utilizing the TRACTr technology to target patients with solid tumors. I also mentioned the prospects of Camzyos as well, which was approved back in April of 2022 to treat adults with symptomatic New York Heart Association (NYHA) class II-III obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy [oHCM]. This drug is continuing to do well because in Q4 of 2025, it was noted that it had generated worldwide sales of $353 million, which ended up being a percentage growth of 59% from the same quarter in the prior year. One major issue is that this company is facing patent cliffs in the coming years, and this would be in terms of its Legacy Portfolio of products. This includes drugs like Revlimid, Eliquis, Pomalyst, Abraxane, Sprycel, and more. The last time around I had a "Strong Buy" rating on this company, and I'm going to maintain this rating. There are several reasons why I'm going to do so.

The first reason is because the company already has a strategy in place to counter the lost revenues due to the patent cliffs set to come in the next several years. The goal is to counter lost revenues from the Legacy Portfolio with what the company now calls the Growth Portfolio. The main thing to note here is that the Growth Portfolio now accounts for roughly more than 50% of its total revenues. That, plus it is continuing to grow at a double-digit percentage rate. In Q4 of 2025, the Growth Portfolio generated $7.4 billion in revenues, which is crucial because this ended up being a 16% year-over-year [YoY] increase. This was driven by Camzyos for sure, along with other drugs like Breyanzi, Opdualag, Reblozyl, and several others. As long as this keeps growing the way it is, then it will definitely counter the losses from the patent cliffs the company faces.

The second reason is because the company is looking to find a new drug that could become a successor to that of Revlimid and/or Pomalyst. With that being said, the company is hoping that the novel cereblon E3 ligase modulator [CELMoD] iberdomide is going to be able to be a part of the equation to counter losses of the hematology products I just cited directly above. The company is already ahead of the game, as a planned analysis of MRD negativity rates in the phase 3 EXCALIBER-RRMM study is what allowed it to file for FDA approval. The FDA not only accepted the New Drug Application [NDA] of iberdomide + daratumumab + dexamethasone for review to treat these patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma [r/r MM] but also has set a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of August 17, 2026. Secondly, there are a few data readouts from registrational studies of Milvexian. The beauty of this company is that it also has an early-stage readout of dual-targeting BCMAxGPRC5D CAR T-cell therapy BMS-986453 for the treatment of patients with r/r MM. Lastly, there is a CELMoD drug known as mezigdomide, which is also a protein degrader of transcription factors of Ikaros and Aiolos to kill cancer cells. This was explored in the phase 3 SUCCESSOR-2 study treating patients with r/r MM.

The third and final reason is because of the drug it has known as Cobenfy. This drug was approved for the treatment of patients with Schizophrenia. The thing to note here is that this might be a "pipeline in a drug" product. That's because Bristol-Myers Squibb is set to release data from the ADEPT 1, 2 & 4 studies using this drug to target patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD] Psychosis in 2026. From there, there are data readouts between 2027/2028 of Cobenfy for the treatment of patients with Bipolar I disorder, AD agitation, and AD cognition. The point here is that the company still has avenues to counter its patent losses that are coming in the next several years. Consider that the potential peak revenue projection for iberdomide alone [with combinations and also expanding to earlier lines of therapies] could bring in peak annual revenue between $5 billion and $6 billion. It just needs to receive FDA approval of iberdomide and achieve wins in its pipeline. With Growth Portfolio revenues continuing to grow 16% YoY, plus the ability to recapture losses from Revlimid/Pomalyst with iberdomide and mezigdomide, I believe that this stock should retain a "Strong Buy" rating.

CELMoDs Iberdomide And Mezigdomide Set Stage For Next Wave Of Growth To Counter Losses Of Revlimid

As I mentioned above, a major problem that Bristol-Myers Squibb faces is the loss of patents for its drug Revlimid. However, the beauty of this company is that it is already looking at countering this issue with its next-generation CELMoD agents, iberdomide and mezigdomide, for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. As a matter of fact, it already obtained positive results from its phase 3 EXCALIBER-RRMM study evaluating the use of this drug to treat these patients. Before going over this clinical study in detail, plus any catalysts to be released from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Multiple Myeloma [MM] is characterized as a cancer of white blood cells [plasma cells]. The thing is that plasma cells are crucial for producing proteins [antibodies] that are responsible for fighting off infections for patients. In this type of cancer, the abnormal cells crowd out the healthy cells for starters. Plus, they produce abnormal antibodies [M-proteins] that cause damage to the kidneys, bones, and generation of blood cells. There is an effect on multiple aspects of the body with this disorder:

  • Reduction of necessary blood cells can cause anemia
  • M-protein buildup causes damage to kidneys and other organs
  • Myeloma cancer cells activate other nearby cells, which can damage the bone, leading to bone pain

The global multiple myeloma market size is projected to reach $44.19 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity for sure; however, it is important to consider that the company is looking to use iberdomide for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma [r/r MM]. With that being said, in order for a patient to have relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma, they have to either relapse [cancer comes back] or be refractory [stop responding to treatment] within 60 days of having received treatment. This is a devastating type of cancer because of r/r disease. It is believed that nearly all MM patients will eventually relapse in disease. The hope for this company is that its next generation of protein degraders, iberdomide and mezigdomide, will help improve outcomes for these patients. Plus, they are expected to be more potent than Revlimid as IMiDs [immunomodulatory imide drugs].

In order to see whether or not iberdomide would be able to help treat these patients with r/r MM, the company ran the multicenter, two-stage, randomized, open-label phase 3 EXCALIBER-RRMM study. This particular study was set up in two different stages: Stage 1 and Stage 2. In Stage 1 of this late-stage trial, 200 patients were randomized as follows:

  • 1 mg iberdomide + daratumumab + dexamethasone [IberDd]
  • 1.3 mg IberDd
  • 1.6 mg IberDd
  • Daratumumab, bortezomib, and dexamethasone [DVd]

Okay, so the point here is that the company picked the move-forward dose of 1 mg of iberdomide for Stage 2. Stage 2 then randomized a total of 664 patients 1:1 as follows:

  • 332 patients in treatment arm A [IberDd]
  • 332 patients in treatment arm B [DVd]

The beauty of this program is that the company did indeed observe that iberdomide as a CELMoD agent was able to help treat these patients. Specifically, it was revealed that this drug in combination with daratumumab + dexamethasone achieved a statistically significant improvement of minimal residual disease [MRD] negativity rates compared to the control arm. The thing to note here is that this trial is still continuing on, and that is because there is another endpoint that must eventually be evaluated, and this is progression-free survival [PFS].

The main thing to note is that there might be a "first" achieved if FDA approval is granted. It is possible that iberdomide could become the first CELMoD agent to ever be approved. The main premise is that this builds on what the company has been able to do with IMiDs. Why is that? That's because CELMoDs are more potent and have been built to overcome resistance observed with that of IMiDs like Revlimid/Pomalyst. This program is coming along well, and one reason for my "Strong Buy" rating is because there is a major inflection point this year. The FDA had not only accepted the NDA submission of iberdomide + standard therapies [daratumumab + dexamethasone] for the treatment of these patients with r/r MM but also set a Priority Review PDUFA date of August 17, 2026.

The thing is that this company also has been able to achieve positive results with the other CELMoD agent, known as mezigdomide. This would be the fact that it had announced positive results from the phase 3 SUCCESSOR-2 study, using oral mezigdomide in combination with carfilzomib and dexamethasone [MeziKd] for the treatment of patients with r/r MM. Specifically, the interim analysis showed that the mezigdomide combination was able to achieve a statistically significant improvement of PFS versus carfilzomib and dexamethasone alone [KD]. The point is that despite current available therapies for these patients, mezigdomide might help in terms of preventing relapses for these patients. That's because these patients don't do well when only given an anti-CD38 and lenalidomide.

Cobenfy Is Another Drug To Stabilize Revenues With Looming Patent Cliffs

The second reason for maintaining the "Strong Buy" rating is because the company has already received FDA approval of Cobenfy for the treatment of patients with Schizophrenia. In 2025, this drug was able to generate sales of $155 million, which is a good start. However, this portion of potential I'm about to go over needs to deliver like the iberdomide/mezigdomide candidates above. The reason why is because Bristol-Myers Squibb is looking to expand this drug to several other indications. As I mentioned beforehand, this would be in terms of targeting patients with Bipolar I disorder, AD agitation, and AD cognition. Data from these specific indications are going to be spread throughout 2027/2028. However, what I want to go over here is the program of developing Cobenfy for the treatment of patients with AD psychosis.

The reason why is because there are several studies ongoing at the present time [ADEPT-1, 2 & 4], and clinical data from this program is expected to be released in 2026. Alzheimer's Disease [AD] is a progressive brain disorder where memory, thinking, and skills are eroded over time. Brain cells that die over time are the cause of this. Why does it happen? It is believed that a buildup of amyloid plaque and neurofibrillary tangles causes such destruction of said cells. The global Alzheimer's therapeutic market size is projected to reach $15.19 billion by 2030. This marks a very large market opportunity, and the hope is that this company can go after the entire market eventually.

The thing is that the ADEPT program studies are targeting patients with AD psychosis. These are the clinical studies of ADEPT-1, ADEPT-2, and ADEPT-4. This program was expected to read out data earlier, but site irregularities prompted the company to remove patients from primary analysis. In consultation with the FDA, plus the interim analysis conducted by the Data Monitoring Committee [DMC], it was decided that additional patients should be recruited. To this end, data from these studies are expected to be available by the end of 2026. This was highly anticipated data, and rightly so, because it is believed that approximately 50% of patients with AD have psychosis. Thus, this is a huge inflection point here for this program if the company can achieve positive results from its phase 3 ADEPT program using Cobenfy to treat them.

Financials

The company still has work to do for sure, because it needs to continue to counter its revenue loss of the Legacy Portfolio of products it has. In Q4 of 2025, it squeaked out with a revenue percentage increase of 1% to $12.5 billion. What's important to take away from this number is that there is a positive shift in the momentum of what I described above, which is that it is shifting its revenues from the Legacy Portfolio over to its Growth Portfolio. This point can be proven with the fact that it reported revenues for this specific Portfolio to be $7.4 billion in Q4 of 2025 [Bristol-Myers Squibb Q4 2025 Results, February 5, 2026, slide #8]. Furthermore, the significance of this is that this ended up being an increase of 16% compared to the same time period in the prior year.

The thing is that it is near having 60% of its Growth Portfolio as part of its total revenues, and thus I believe it is on the right track to achieving its strategic goal of countering the loss of patents for key products. The key thing to watch going forward is the next earnings report that the company is to release. Specifically, if there is still significant growth in the Growth Portfolio. This momentum can be seen soon enough, because Bristol-Myers Squibb is expected to report its Q1 of 2026 earnings results on April 30, 2026, before the market opens at 8:00 AM EST. The expectation for Q1 of 2026 is that it will report an EPS of between $1.45 and $1.46, and this would be a decline from the year prior. In addition, revenue is expected to be slightly lower, by 2%–2.3%, being that of between $10.85 and $10.94 billion, respectively.

The premise here is that the company is expected to have a significant second half of 2026 because of multiple data readouts from its pipeline. This is the reason for my "Strong Buy" rating. The main thing here is for it to execute on iberdomide/mezigdomide in the treatment of patients with r/r MM. Along with the execution of the other candidates I went over above. Plus, there are two data readouts in 2026 from a candidate known as milvexian, which is being evaluated in the phase 3 LIBREXIA-AF1 study treating patients with atrial fibrillation [AF] and the phase 3 LIBREXIA-STROKE1 trial for secondary stroke prevention [SSP]. Lastly, the company has given higher guidance for 2026 as well [Bristol-Myers Squibb Q4 2025 Results, February 5, 2026, slide #15]. It notes the ability to report full-year 2026 revenues to be between $46 billion and $47.5 billion.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Bristol-Myers Squibb. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its Growth Portfolio. As I mentioned above, it is on the right track, with nearly 60% of its total revenues being comprised of its Growth Portfolio of products. This is evidenced by not only this fact but also that this specific Portfolio grew by 16% to $7.4 billion in Q4 of 2025. The goal here is that this big pharma really needs to continue to achieve growth in this category because its Legacy Portfolio is faltering. For full-year 2026, it expects a decline in this Portfolio of between 12% and 16%. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will be able to effectively counter the loss of patents in the next several years with this Growth Portfolio. Especially if the data readouts end up being negative, and the company can't rely on such new products. In the most recently reported Q4 of 2025, its immuno-oncology [IO] portfolio, Camzyos, Breyanzi, and Reblozyl were the ones that helped it to achieve growth. There is no guarantee that one or more of these products are going to achieve a similar or superior growth pattern in the coming quarters.

The second risk involves that of the expansion opportunity I highlighted above, which is the development of iberdomide for the treatment of patients with r/r MM. That's because the company not only submitted the NDA of this drug for the treatment of these patients but was also accepted as Priority Review status with a PDUFA date of August 17, 2026. If this drug is approved by the FDA, it holds the potential to become the first CELMoD agent to have achieved this feat. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to grant U.S. marketing approval of this drug for the treatment of these r/r MM patients. In addition, the notion here for this company is also to advance the use of its other CELMoD agent, mezigdomide, for this very same patient population. Data from the phase 3 SUCCESSOR-1 study using mezigdomide + bortezomib + dexamethasone [MeziVd] to treat these patients is expected in 2027. Even though the company achieved positive results from its phase 3 SUCCESSOR-2 study using mezigdomide in combination with carfilzomib and dexamethasone [MeziKd], there is no assurance that positive results will be obtained for the phase 3 SUCCESSOR-1 study using a different combination of MeziVd.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of Cobenfy for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD] Psychosis in the ongoing phase 3 ADEPT-1, ADEPT-2, & ADEPT-4 studies. The company needs a win here, because this would greatly expand this first-in-class, non-dopaminergic antipsychotic to target another large patient population. Data from these studies are expected to be released by the end of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will release positive data from this study for starters. Even if positive data is obtained from this trial and a regulatory filing occurs, there is no guarantee that the FDA is going to accept this application for review. Nor that Bristol-Myers Squibb is going to be able to expand the label of Cobenfy to be able to treat patients with AD Psychosis.

Conclusion

Bristol-Myers Squibb definitely still has some work to do for sure. That's because it is expected to potentially see a small dip in revenue for its Q1 of 2026. On the flip side, it has given higher full-year 2026 guidance for itself. In my opinion, what happens next with this company is going to hinge on its Growth Portfolio. That is, it needs to see continued revenue growth for this Portfolio to offset that of its Legacy Portfolio. Much more than that, as I have gone over above, it still has candidates in place to help it with this matter. The biggest problem would be losing so much revenue from Revlimid and Pomalyst.

However, I believe that it is on the right track because it is already in the process of further enhancing the targeting of patients with r/r MM. That is, moving from its IMiDs I just mentioned [with expected declining sales] over to that of CELMoDs iberdomide and mezigdomide. As I laid out in the beginning, iberdomide could bring in peak annual revenues for this company of between $5 and $6 billion. With an increase in revenues of its Growth Portfolio of 16% to $7.4 billion, plus the ability to potentially continue this trend going forward with candidates like iberdomide, mezigdomide, and milvexian, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating. Should the FDA decline to approve iberdomide for r/r MM and many of the data readouts turn poor in the 2nd half of 2026, then I will reevaluate the stock again at such time.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lord Abbett Ultra Short Bond Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The first quarter was defined by a balance between economic resilience and rising geopolitical and macro uncertainty. Market performance faced headwinds from weakness in Technology/Software (driven by AI disruption concerns) and escalating geopolitical tensions, though supportive macroeconomic conditions helped offset some of the pressure. Labor market signals were mixed and inflation remained above target, but expectations for earnings growth, fiscal stimulus, and eventual tariff stabilization helped anchor the outlook. Late in the quarter, geopolitical risks intensified as tensions in the Middle East disrupted energy markets and pushed oil prices higher, delaying expectations for rate cuts and introducing near-term inflation risks. Despite this backdrop, the underlying economy remained constructive, supported by solid consumer spending, continued earnings momentum, and expectations for longer-term policy support.
  • Over the quarter, short-term investment grade corporate 2 credit spreads widened by 11 basis points (bps), short-term commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads tightened by 4 bps, and short-term asset backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads remained flat. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.48% to 3.79% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield moved higher from 4.17% to 4.32% 1 .

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned 0.75%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended March 31 st , 2026. The Fund's benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. Treasury Bill Index*, returned 0.86% during the same period.
  • The portfolio's allocation to fixed-rate corporate bonds detracted from relative performance. Over the quarter, we increased the portfolio's allocation to fixed-rate corporate bonds, buying into higher yields during the volatility.
  • There were no other significant detractors over the period.
  • The portfolio's allocation to commercial paper was the primary contributor to relative performance given the sector's greater carry over Treasury bills. Over the quarter, we decreased the portfolio's allocation to commercial paper to reallocate to sectors with more attractive relative value.
  • The portfolio's allocation to asset backed securities (ABS) also contributed to relative performance. Over the period, we maintained our exposure to ABS.

Performance as of 03/31/2026

1Q26 1YR 3 YR 5YR Since Inception**
Net Asset Value (I Share) (net) 0.75% 4.50% 5.44% 3.55% 2.73%
ICE BofA U.S. Treasury Bill Index * 0.86% 4.11% 4.81% 3.37% 2.39%
Morningstar Ultra-Short Category Fund Average 5 0.73% 4.34% 5.28% 3.39% -

Gross Expense Ratio: 0.29% **inception date 10/17/2016

*The ICE BofA U.S. Treasury Bill Index tracks the performance of US dollar denominated US Treasury Bills publicly issued in the US domestic market. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and an investor cannot invest directly in an index.

Performance data quoted reflect past performance and are no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance quoted. The investment return and principal value of an investment in the Fund will fluctuate so that shares, on any given day or when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. You can obtain performance data current to the most recent month end by calling Lord Abbett at (888) 522-2388 or referring to our website at Welcome | Lord, Abbett & Co LLC | Lord Abbett.

The performance table above is based on Class I shares. Average Annual Total Returns are based on changes in the net asset value and assume reinvestment of all distributions, and do not reflect deduction of any front-end sales charges which are not applicable for Class I shares. Returns for less than one year are not annualized.

1 Factset as of 03/31/2026

2 As represented by the ICE BofA US Corporate (1-3 Y) Index as of 03/31/2026

3 As represented by the Bloomberg US CMBS Investment Grade (1-3.5 Y) Index as of 03/31/2026

4 As represented by the Bloomberg US ABS Composite (1-3 Y) Index as of 03/31/2026

5 Ultrashort-bond portfolios within the Morningstar Ultra-Short Category invest primarily in investment-grade U.S. fixed-income issues and have durations typically of less than one year. This category can include corporate or government ultrashort bond portfolios, but it excludes international, convertible, multisector, and high-yield bond portfolios. Because of their focus on bonds with very short durations, these portfolios offer minimal interest-rate sensitivity and therefore low risk and total return potential. data.title: Metro: Still A Buy Despite Strike-Driven Headwinds In Laval
data.description:
I told you that Santa Claus left us two post-Christmas setups in big grocers, Metro (MRU:CA) and Tesco (TSCDY).

The British giant ended up paying well (~20% in four months, while the S&P only increased by 2% in the same stretch), even maintaining its strategy that is allowing them to gain market share.

Metro, on the other hand, fell a bit short for me.

Don't get me wrong, the quarter itself wasn't bad. But it seems the Grinch stole our February gains with a post-earnings slump in Q2 FY 2026.

And here it was the same headwind I flagged at Dino Polska (DNOPY): strikes.

Of course, nobody was expecting this, and I'll probably have to put some impact—both on sales and margins—for the whole FY 2026.

But is that total return of ~20% still there for us to grab? What I had in mind was ~8–15% upside, which, with tasty dividends (Metro is a Dividend Aristocrat!) and buybacks, gets you there.

Best place to start is the quarter itself.

If it came in better than expected, a good chunk of that market fear should fade.

If It Weren’t for the Strike…

I'll be honest, I tend to overthink things.

It helps when you're digging into a stock, no doubt. But once you've got too many scenarios running in your head at the same time, it starts working against you.

The catch is, I genuinely think that without the strike, the stock probably pops mid-single digits right after earnings.

Doubt it? Well, I have some arguments for that.

To begin with, they pulled a nice beat for revenue ($5.1 billion, beating almost $35 million of what Wall St. was targeting) while keeping EPS in line.

Here's a look at it:

And if you take into account that they were still dealing with the restructuring of Toronto's DC, that's actually pretty solid. I wasn’t expecting high single-digit growth, but ~4% came in above what I had for the full year (~3%) in the last piece.

But like I always say in grocery write-ups, you've got to look past the headline.

For example, same-store food sales were only ~1.8%, with slightly negative traffic. That is, Metro is still leaning more on new stores and some price points to drive that 4.1% sales growth.

Pharmacies chipped in with a ~5.1% bump, but at the end of the day it's still a grocery business at its core.

And it's not just Metro that's going through this. Many chains (American and non-American) like Ahold Delhaize (ADRNY), Weis Markets (WMK), Village (VLGEA) are seeing this trade-off of fewer customers but slightly fuller shopping carts.

Here, fresh food options, in-store dining, and many other tricks work to try to alleviate the pressure from hard-discounters like Aldi and Lidl.

But remember that just like Loblaw (L:CA), Metro also operates discount banners!

Losing traffic to Aldi in Quebec with Super-C is not good at all.

Here's a peek at same-store sales for the big three in Canada:

Everyone’s more or less in the same boat.

Hard discounters are probably doing a bit better than traditional stores, like the Loblaw CEO pointed out on the last call, but the biggest chunk of stores are the traditional ones.

And, to tell you the truth, I still think this is something sticky for the average Canadian.

I say this because Ipsos’ Consumer Confidence is still edging down month-over-month, even if the drops are getting smaller, and wage growth isn’t really moving. Add in higher beef and coffee prices, and it makes sense folks keep trading down.

None of these sales are new; it's been the same pattern for at least a couple of years now.

On what they can actually control on the bottom line, they did a pretty decent job this quarter.

To get into that, let’s start with margins:

Take adjusted EBITDA, for example. Metro reported it at $488.5 million (a margin of ~9.6% in the quarter, above the 5-year average) thanks to a bump in gross margin (likely from a stronger mix of private labels and DC normalizing) and SG&A ~10 bp lower.

Add that to the buybacks (at least 8.6 million shares purchased since Q2 FY 2025), and you'll see an 8.8% bump in adjusted EPS to $1.11. That's what you'd call lower-quality earnings; most of the EPS lift isn't really coming from operations.

But honestly, this was to be expected (and even desired for income-oriented investors) in defensive stocks.

On top of that, Metro is still running a clean balance sheet (Debt/EBITDA ~2.2x) and throwing off solid FCF, around ~$490 million just this quarter.

It's a mature grocer, for sure.

Now the big question is how much the Laval DC strike eats into that.

Modeling Some Scenarios for the Strike

I've put together a few scenarios below to give you a sense of what the impact could look like before it even reaches Wall St.

It all starts with looking at Quebec.

That’s Metro’s home (~80% of stores), so out of roughly $22.7 billion in FY 2026 sales, you’re looking at about $18 billion tied to the province.

Although Metro doesn't release specific figures for fresh products, I'll estimate them as representing a quarter of sales here.

Adjusting it further for the impact of 300 stores surrounding Laval (that's the size of the local disruption some sources tell us), the annualized affected sales would end up at just over $1.3 billion.

I’m working with ~25% impact on that chunk (~$325 million annualized) and then scaling that across a few time scenarios:

I’m just using ~1.5x as part of the halo effect.

That gives you an estimated revenue hit of ~$39 million (1 month), ~$78 million (2 months), and ~$122 million (3 months).

If I then look back at my projection of $22.7 billion in revenue for FY 2026, it would fall back to something close to $22.6 billion in the best-case scenario and $22.5 billion in the worst-case scenario.

Even if it lands somewhere in between, I'll stick with $22.5 billion to stay on the conservative side. Street's still at ~$22.7 billion, unchanged.

It's not that much, don't you think?

But the biggest blow comes at the bottom line.

Market consensus already seems to have priced that in a bit, inching down EPS from $5.61 to $5.57.

I'd rather focus on EBITDA here. Simpler and easier when dealing with grocery stores.

Applying ~1.5–2x to the base case (2 months), you're looking at a bit over ~$135 million in EBITDA headwind. That is, if the EBITDA midpoint would be $2.1 billion in my last article, with the most likely strike scenario, this would be pushed to $1.96 billion.

And here it really makes a big difference.

Previously, EBITDA was projected to grow ~12.9% year-over-year, but with these headwinds, it should inch up only 5.3%.

What Changes in My Price Target?

Using the same model from my last article, just updating the inputs (EBITDA at $1.96 billion and ~208–209 million shares outstanding, assuming ~3% buyback yield), I get a price target in the $97.50–$98 range.

That’s ~3.6% lower in the best-case scenario than the midpoint of my previous calculation.

It's still an upside of at least 7% from the $91 the stock is trading at today.

Looking at this, the total return here seems to have fallen from that 15% in the best-case scenario to something more like ~10 to 11%.

To me, this setup still looks like a 'Buy'.

No longer the high-conviction type, but more geared towards income-oriented investors who like to buy defensive stocks at the bottom with some upside.

Metro is still a good stock for income lovers, especially paying more consistent dividends than any other Canadian grocer (and here I include Loblaw!).

data.title: Parnassus Mid Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • The Fund (Investor Shares) returned -2.35% (net of fees) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell Midcap Index's 1.29% return.
  • Fund performance was hampered by our holdings in Industrials, Materials and Health Care, as well as a lack of exposure to Energy. Performance was helped by our holdings in the Financials and Consumer Staples sectors.
  • In the first quarter, we took advantage of market volatility to upgrade the portfolio with companies we view as stronger growth prospects as we position for broadening market opportunities.
  • We remain focused on selecting companies with durable growth characteristics, which we believe are well equipped to navigate heightened geopolitical risks and the potential disruptions posed by artificial intelligence (AI).

Market Review

The Russell Midcap Index added to 2025's strong return with a modest gain in the first quarter, as market leadership broadened away from large cap technology stocks into more reasonably valued mid caps amid heightened market volatility from geopolitical shocks and shifting AI narratives. Equity markets reversed course in the first quarter of 2026, as early optimism surrounding the ongoing megatrend in AI and easier monetary policy yielded to geopolitical shocks and tighter financial conditions. While the AI megatrend remained intact, fears of AI disruption caused sharp sell-offs among high-growth technology, software and information services stocks. The Federal Reserve held interest rates steady due to persistent inflation and economic uncertainty. The conflict with Iran drove oil prices sharply higher and resulted in a shift to a risk-off market sentiment, which pressured global equities and sharply increased volatility. During the quarter, the Energy sector's strong returns led the Russell Midcap Index, followed by healthy but more modest returns in the Materials and Utilities sectors. The Communication Services, Financials and Health Care sectors ended the quarter with losses.

Quarterly Performance Review

Underperformance partially offset by strength in Financials and Consumer Staples

The Fund (Investor Shares) returned -2.35% (net of fees) for the quarter, underperforming the Russell Midcap Index's 1.29% return. Performance was hampered by our holdings in the Industrials, Materials and Health Care sectors, along with lack of exposure to the Energy sector. Conversely, performance was helped by our holdings in the Financials and Consumer Staples sectors, along with a lack of exposure to the Communication Services sector.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Sandisk, a global leader in flash memory solutions, continued to rally as tight memory supply conditions and robust demand from data center and AI workloads supported a favorable pricing environment.

Lam Research (LRCX), a designer and maker of semiconductor processing equipment, continued to benefit from demand for its industry-leading process tools and equipment, driven by the accelerating build-out of AI infrastructure.

KLA (KLAC), a provider of process control and yield management semiconductor equipment, saw sustained demand tied to AI-driven semiconductor capital spending.

US Foods Holding (USFD) delivered strong performance after the food supplier and distributor issued a bullish full-year 2026 outlook, supported by continued market share gains and reduced costs due to AI improvements.

Old Dominion (ODFL) Freight Line demonstrated resilience from freight demand recovery and early signals of industrial stabilization. The company also stands to benefit from the Trump administration's manufacturing and reshoring efforts.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

BILL Holdings (BILL) shares underperformed amid continued uncertainty about small and midsize business spending, compounded by investor worries over AI's potential to disrupt its payments platform.

CBRE Group (CBRE) shares declined as commercial real estate sentiment weakened due to higher financing costs and a cautious lending environment. Fears that AI could disrupt the company's traditional commercial real estate brokerage and advisory models also pressured shares.

Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR) shares detracted amid AI disruption risks to the firm's traditional businesses, while management flagged a lengthening sales cycle for new client mandates amid broader economic caution.

TransUnion (TRU) shares dropped amid investor fears that AI will disrupt the firm's traditional credit reporting and data analytics models. Management also issued disappointing 2026 guidance, due to ongoing sluggishness in the U.S. housing industry and international headwinds.

Autodesk (ADSK) shares dropped amid fears that AI will disrupt the firm's traditional design and engineering software business, while management flagged rising macroeconomic uncertainty in the first quarter.

Portfolio Positioning

Balancing growth, quality and resiliency

In the first quarter, we continued to sharpen the portfolio's focus on faster-growing, competitively advantaged businesses without materially increasing valuation risk. In a market characterized by rising uncertainty, especially due to the Iran war and investor concerns about AI disruption risks, we identified opportunities to upgrade the portfolio toward more relevant businesses with stronger growth prospects while maintaining our emphasis on balance sheet strength, margin durability and downside resilience.

Within Consumer Discretionary, we initiated positions in Royal Caribbean Cruises and Dick's Sporting Goods. (DKS) Royal Caribbean is a leading global cruise operator with a differentiated fleet and demonstrated pricing power, benefiting from continued demand for experiences and favorable industry dynamics. Dick's Sporting Goods continues to gain share through scale advantages, with the Foot Locker acquisition providing an additional lever for growth.

In Industrials, we reinitiated a position in CNH Industrial (CNH), a global manufacturer of agricultural and construction equipment. While the agricultural cycle remains subdued, we believe CNH is well positioned to benefit from an eventual recovery, with additional upside from ongoing operational improvements.

In Health Care, we initiated a position in Medline, a scaled medical-surgical distributor with a resilient business model supported by recurring demand, steady growth and visibility through a multiyear customer backlog.

To fund these additions, we exited Workday and Roper Technologies, where AI-driven shifts in customer spending introduce greater competition and pressure on legacy enterprise software growth models.

We exited Ares Management, a private credit investment firm, as recent liquidity pressures and credit concerns at several high-profile borrowers have increased scrutiny on the asset class and dampened fundraising and investor demand. We exited IQVIA Holdings and Alcon (ALC) due to concerns about slowing growth within our investment horizon.

Overall, the portfolio remains concentrated in companies with durable competitive advantages, pricing power, strong balance sheets and consistent free cash flow generation, where we see a clearer path to compounding earnings across cycles. We continue to balance exposure to secular growth themes, including AI infrastructure and electrification, with selective opportunities tied to cyclical improvement, positioning the Fund to compound returns across a range of market environments.

Outlook

Cautiously optimistic on mid cap equities despite uncertainty

While investors began the year expecting robust growth, moderating inflation and lower rates, those assumptions were challenged during the first quarter as macro uncertainty increased and geopolitical tensions rose. We enter the second quarter with mid cap equities navigating a more uncertain environment in which the market has been responding to shifting narratives rather than changing fundamentals.

AI is likely to remain a key driver of market returns, but it is also reshaping competitive dynamics across multiple industries. In areas such as software and data services, investors are increasingly focused on potential pressures on pricing and switching costs. Seemingly overnight, companies previously viewed as having wide competitive moats are now under scrutiny. The result is meaningful dispersion, as the market sorts companies into AI beneficiaries and those increasingly exposed to disruption from AI-driven changes in their industries.

At the same time, higher energy prices, driven by geopolitical developments, are compounding these pressures and reinforcing a K-shaped environment, where a narrow group of companies benefit from strong secular tailwinds such as AI adoption and energy demand, while others face more muted conditions.

Looking out to the rest of 2026, we're still relatively optimistic that economic growth could broaden beyond AI. We're encouraged by the broadening opportunity set, particularly in companies gaining market share with identifiable growth drivers such as pricing power, product innovation or exposure to expanding end markets. We remain disciplined, fundamentals-driven investors focused on durable businesses at reasonable valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

data.title: Chipotle Q1 2026 Results Preview: It's All About Transactions Growth
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous article on Chipotle (CMG), I discussed my long-term views on the stock and how I see the current slump in the share price as a rare entry point into this strong cash-generating company. Chipotle’s growth path has been temporarily disrupted by weak economic conditions that are putting pressure on consumers’ wallets. Management rolled out a new plan, which they called the Recipe for Growth. The purpose behind it is to respond to current market challenges and boost revenue over the medium term. For this article, I am focusing on the Q1 '26 earnings results scheduled for April 29, 2026, and what the main puts and takes are going into the print.

Chipotle's share price has so far underperformed the market in 2026. The stock declined 7.5% on a YTD basis, while the S&P 500 gained 4.7% over the same period. This follows a massive underperformance in 2025, where CMG declined by 39% compared to a 16.4% gain in the S&P 500. The sentiment on Chipotle soured in 2026 following the Q4 25 earnings release, when management provided guidance for flat comparable sales growth, which was well below the 1.9% increase the market was anticipating.

Figure 1: YTD Share Price Performance

The Recipe for Growth and What It Means for Q1

During the Q4 2025 earnings call, management outlined its strategy to accelerate growth and sharpen competitiveness, formally called the "Recipe for Growth." This strategy is built on 5 pillars:

  1. Drive Operational and Culinary Excellence: This is largely focused on rolling out the high-efficiency equipment package (HEEP). This new equipment reduces the time needed to cook food by 2-3 hours. This enables stores to increase throughput during peak hours and achieve higher labor efficiencies. At the start of 2026, Chipotle had rolled out HEEP to 300 stores and aims to have 2,000 stores equipped with HEEP by the end of the year.
  2. Accelerate Demand Through Brand Messaging, Menu Innovation, and New Occasions: This is a multi-pronged pillar that aims to accelerate the cadence of menu innovation to four LTOs in 2026. Also, the company plans to launch new sauces and lean into high-protein offerings. Moreover, it aims to increase awareness of group orders, including Build Your Own Chipotle and catering.
  3. Leverage AI and Relaunch the Rewards Program: Chipotle aims to increase rewards program penetration from its current level of 30%. The focus will be on in-store purchases, where only 20% of the transactions flow through the rewards platform (compared to nearly 90% on the app).
  4. Expand Global Reach: Chipotle aims to reach 7,000 stores in North America and expand its international presence, with partner-operated stores in some new markets.
  5. Cultivate the Best Talent in the Industry: Chipotle's talent strategy centers on promoting from within and adding external hires to accelerate growth.

What are the implications of the Recipe for Growth on Q1?

  • HEEP rollout will have started to pick up some momentum, likely doubling the number of stores equipped with it. However, it is unlikely to have any noticeable impact on Q1 results as it is still in the early stages.
  • The protein menu was rolled out in December 2025, and this should provide a sales boost in Q1. Also, the LTO Chicken Al Pastor was rolled out on February 10, 2026, and this is well ahead of the Honey Chicken last year (which was rolled out on March 7, 2025). So there should be a month of positive LTO demand pull. Also, the company launched the Cilantro Lime sauce on March 19, 2026. Though this sauce might prove to be a hit with customers and contribute to increasing the average ticket price, it will only have around 12 days of sales impact during the quarter (making it more of a Q2 driver).
  • Chipotle announced the relaunch of its rewards program on April 13, 2026. This will have no bearing on Q1 results given that it falls outside the quarter. Moreover, the relaunch of the rewards programs feels more like a tweak. This makes it unlikely to be a major driver of user engagement without further refinements.

Figure 2: Chipotle’s Recipe for Growth

Street Expectations at a Glance

For the Q1 '26 print, the Street is setting a relatively low bar for CMG. The consensus revenue forecast is $3.06 billion, representing just a 6.4% YoY increase. The consensus EPS is $0.24, and that implies a 17% YoY decline. Analysts are factoring in continued pressure on comparable store sales and rising ingredient costs (management called out higher prices for avocados, beef, and cooking oils in the Q4 25 earnings call). Also, the company had guided for rising labor costs and higher G&A expenses as a percentage of sales. Thus, with modest revenue growth and rising costs, all the forecasts on the Street point to double-digit declines in EPS.

Figure 3: Consensus Forecast for Q1 '26

Store Network Expansion

For FY26, Chipotle is guiding for 350 to 370 new restaurant openings, which include 10 to 15 international partner-operated restaurants. Thus, the company aims to open 340 to 355 new company-operated restaurants in FY26. At the lower end, this is slightly ahead of the 334 new restaurants it opened in FY25. Figure 4 shows the cadence of new store openings for the last 8 quarters. Q1 usually has the fewest new restaurant openings. So, it is very likely that Chipotle will open around 60 restaurants in Q1 (a level that matches its previous run rate).

Assuming Chipotle opens 60 new stores in Q1 '26, the net number of stores opened over the last 4 quarters will be 321 (adjusted for closures and relocations). Accordingly, I expect Chipotle to end Q1 with 4,102 restaurants, which reflects an increase of 8% YoY.

During the Q4 25 earnings call, management stated that new restaurants generate more than 80% of the sales productivity of the existing asset base. Based on this, I expect new stores to deliver around a 7% revenue uplift in Q1.

Figure 4: New Restaurants per Quarter

Q1 Comparable Sales: Consensus is Still Bracing for Negative Transactions

Management guided to a decline of 1% - 2% in comparable store sales in Q1 '26. This guidance includes a 100 bps negative impact from the massive storm in January. Consensus points to a 6.4% increase in revenue in Q1 '26, but the store network expansion alone should provide a 7% uplift to revenue. Thus, it seems that analysts on the street expect comparable store sales to fall by 0.5% - 1%. This shows that analysts expect comparable store sales to contract in Q1 at a pace below management's guidance.

Management also guided, during the Q4 25 earnings call, for a 0.7% lift from pricing in Q1 '26. Combining the Street’s expectation of a 0.5%-1% decline in comparable store sales with management’s pricing guidance implies that underlying transactions are expected to fall by 1.2%-1.7%. This shows that the street does not expect to see a reversal of the declining transaction trend in Q1 '26. Figure 5 shows that transactions have been falling since Q1 25.

Figure 5: Comparable Store Sales and its Components

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, the stock trades at a forward EV/Sales of 3.8x and a forward P/E of 30.2x, which are below their respective 5-year averages of 5.9x and 51x. This shows that, despite the large correction in the share price, many investors still see strong growth potential for CMG. However, at such multiples, investors need a high degree of conviction in the company’s ability to return to at least mid-single-digit comparable sales growth. So, yes, CMG is trading at a discount to its 5-year average, but its current multiples remain elevated compared to those of its peers. In my view, the growth story still has considerable runway, with ample scope to increase the store network in North America and expand internationally. I am of the view that the current economic situation is presenting the company with a major short-term challenge. However, I believe that its long-term growth story remains intact. Hence, I prefer to focus my valuation on the company’s 5-year average multiples.

Key Risks

The key risk heading into the Q1 print is a downward revision to 2026 guidance, especially for comparable store sales. Chipotle trades at a high valuation, which indicates that investors expect high growth. Many view the current guidance for flat comparable store sales growth in 2026 as a conservative outlook from management. A downward revision in 2026 guidance, though less likely, would be seen as a signal for further deterioration in demand drivers. This would change the narrative on the street and could induce a large selloff. Also, there are inflation-related risks on the cost side, namely ingredient and labor costs, which could impact operating profit margin.

Final Thoughts

Heading into the Q1 '26 print, the risk/reward profile for CMG appears balanced in my view. Expectations are set to a relatively low bar that the company should be able to clear. However, investors will be more focused on transaction numbers and guidance updates. I see a possibility of a small positive surprise in transaction numbers compared to expectations, which would bode well for the recovery story. However, I do not expect to see a major revision in the 2026 guidance, as I expect management will prudently wait for Q2 results before making such a change. Overall, I remain positive on the stock and maintain my Buy rating.

data.title: MaxLinear: Strong Performance, Too Strong Momentum
data.description:
Shares of MaxLinear (MXL) have been absolutely on fire following a strong first-quarter earnings report, accompanied by an even stronger outlook for the second quarter. Momentum has been strong, perhaps too strong, with shares tripling in the time frame of just a week!

This clearly surprised investors, as few signs of such a recovery were visible when I last covered shares in October of last year, at the time a $15 stock, with shares trading at similar levels just a few weeks ago! Of course, the market is clearly surprised and perhaps overreacting to a solid quarter and blowout guidance issued here!

This is driven by momentum in the data center business, which is encouraging based on the current results and improved outlook, yet the pace of momentum feels overextended in my view here.

Other serial special situations and AI beneficiaries are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

About The Reults

MaxLinear reported a sound first-quarter earnings report, and while sequential sales were up just a percent, revenues were up 43% on an annual basis to $137 million. Growth was driven by a 136% increase in infrastructure sales, which has become the largest segment of the business. This was driven by ramped-up production and demand for optical data center products at multiple hyperscale customers.

Infrastructure sales were reported at $63 million, making up nearly half of sales. This is notably complemented by a $4 million revenue contribution from broadband and, to a lesser extent, connectivity revenues and industrial multi-market.

Growth in infrastructure sales is driven by high-speed interconnects. The company furthermore announced a PON design for a large US hyperscale data center, with an expected revenue ramp-up seen in the fourth quarter of the year. CEO Kishore Seendripu calls this a clear inflection point, with further growth and visibility seen for the remainder of 2026.

While the solid topline growth is solid, it comes with a GAAP operating loss of $17 million. This is a remarkable improvement from the first quarter of 2025, yet losses are up modestly from a $15 million number in the fourth quarter of 2025. On a non-GAAP basis, quarterly earnings came in at $0.22 per share, up three cents from the previous quarter, compared to a modest loss a year ago.

Stock-based compensation expenses, which are excluded in these earnings metrics, were reported at $20 million. This is equal to about $0.17 per share after applying statutory taxes, leaving minimal to no realistic earnings.

The company ended the year with net debt of about $33 million, this number being minor in the grand scheme of things. The 88 million shares traded at just $15 by the end of March, for a near $1.4 billion enterprise valuation. With revenues trending well over $500 million, the company traded below 3 times sales, with essentially break-even results reported.

Great Outlook, Great Momentum

Shares have risen from levels in the mid-teens to the $30 mark last week, now having jumped to the $60 mark on the back of the strong first-quarter results, but moreover, a strong outlook. This outlook calls for second-quarter sales between $160 and $170 million, with non-GAAP gross margins seen at a midpoint of 59.5% of sales, with non-GAAP operating expenses reported at $63.5 million.

This compares to a $137 million sales number in the first quarter, similar gross margins, and non-GAAP operating expenses reported at $59.9 million. The outlook suggests about $12 million sequential accretion in non-GAAP operating profits, which could boost sequential profits by nearly twenty cents.

The momentum is strong, with the second quarter revenue outlook at the midpoint seen around $165 million, being well over twenty million ahead of analyst consensus. Moreover, optical data center revenues are seen between $150 and $170 million this year, up from just a $100-$130 million guidance, driven by more demand for its 400G and 800G deployments with its Keystone PAM4 DSP optical receiver platform, used for AI and related workloads. This is not just driven by optical products, with the growth seen pretty widespread, with the business catering to AI and hyperscale clients here.

On top of the spectacular growth, management also touts the improved visibility, with CFO Steven Lichtfield seeing this momentum last into 2027 as well. He commented on the conference call:

with the visibility that we have, we start to roll into '27 as well. I mean, I think we're excited to see the growth in '26 and even backlog starting to build into 2027.

With 88 million shares now trading at $60, the market value has risen to $5.3 billion, for a $5.4 billion enterprise valuation, that number having increased by a factor of nearly 4 times in just the time frame of just a few weeks. Based on the second quarter revenue guidance, revenues trend at around $660 million, pushing up the sales multiple from 3 times to 8 times, pretty much overnight, as a figure of speech.

Of course, earnings are mostly nonexistent at this point, but the sequential improvement makes the earnings quickly run up to a run rate of about a dollar per share, resulting in nosebleed valuations, as expectations have risen substantially.

Some Perspective

The reality is that MaxLinear is far from a start-up, especially catering to the data center boom. Founded in 2003, the company has seen some boom-bust cycles in the past. Most pronounced in these was a share price run to the $70 mark in 2021, at a time when revenues topped a billion and the company obtained too much confidence. After all, it tried to acquire Silicon Motion in a $3.8 billion deal, a transaction that (fortunately) fell apart.

What followed was a legal battle and then a failed but also really painful operating performance, as revenues fell to a run rate of nearly $300 million, with substantial losses reported. While revenues improved a bit in 2025, large realistic losses were reported as investors and management were working for a breakthrough, which is now really taking place.

The question is, of course, how strong this momentum is, as for now the increase in expectations exceeds the increase in performance and even the upbeat guidance here. Still a $20 stock a week ago, the momentum feels bubbly, perhaps almost short-covering driven, with similar moves seen in the wider peer group.

I have no business chasing the shares here, nor do I have an interest in having a short position either, for that matter.

data.title: Artisan High Income Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Discussion

Our portfolio underperformed the ICE BofA US High Yield Index during the quarter. Across asset types, the primary detractors were security selection within the corporate bond component as well as the portfolio's exposure to bank loans, which underperformed the high yield bond benchmark. These effects were partially offset by the portfolio's small exposure to post-reorg equities. By rating, the portfolio benefited from its exposure in unrated debt, while the primary detractors included security selection in B-rated debt, as well as an overweight to and selection within CCC-rated credit. From a sector perspective, the primary contributors included security selection within media and services, while the most notable detractors were the portfolio's exposure and selection in insurance (specifically, insurance brokers) and an underweight in energy.

Investing Environment

Volatility rose across asset classes during the quarter, driven by a combination of geopolitical conflicts, artificial intelligence (AI)-driven disruption fears and private credit concerns. The S&P 500® Index declined 4.3%, led by a significant compression in multiples among software companies; at the end of the quarter, the software segment of the S&P 500® Index was trading at multiples last seen in late 2022 and early 2023. While Treasury yields initially fell in January and February, they jumped sharply in March after the onset of the conflict in Iran. A shock in commodity prices contributed to investors reassessing the path forward for monetary policy, with investor expectations of 2026 rate cuts replaced with the potential for rate hikes—putting upward pressure on Treasury yields.

Relative to equities, credit markets meaningfully outperformed. Both the high yield bond market (as measured by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index) and bank loan market (as measured by the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index) returned -0.5%, driven by spread widening, particularly in lower rated segments. Despite producing similar returns for the quarter, high yield bonds and bank loans took different paths to essentially the same result. Loans initially underperformed high yield bonds during January and February, driven by investor concerns surrounding the loan market's relatively higher exposure to software. As the quarter progressed, high yield bonds subsequently underperformed, driven by the sharp increase in Treasury yields in March.

During the quarter, software exposure became a notable topic of discussion across risk assets, including credit markets. Increasing fears of AI-driven obsolescence for legacy software companies have highlighted the large differences in software exposure across segments of credit. While exposure varies significantly across markets, high yield bonds are estimated to have the least amount of software risk, while private credit is estimated to be the most exposed as measured by business development company (BDC) exposure to the sector. Importantly, while passive vehicles investing in the high yield and loan markets aim to closely match index exposures, skilled active managers can position portfolios to take advantage of software dislocation by avoiding “bad” (i.e., weaker positioned) software assets while upsizing exposure to “good” (i.e., durable and mission-critical) software assets.

Elsewhere, private credit issues dominated headlines, with a significant uptick in redemptions during the quarter from investors in semi-liquid vehicles such as private business development companies (BDCs). Driven by increasing investor concerns surrounding liquidity, transparency and software exposure, many private (nontraded) BDCs received redemption requests above their typical 5% limit, with some choosing to provide additional capital above the requisite amount. As our investors are aware, we have discussed for years the potential for credit stress arising from 2020 and 2021 vintage direct lending deals, where lending standards loosened after a rush of capital invested into this corner of the market. This period can be summarized with many transactions occurring at tight spreads, base rates near zero and many companies “over-earning” during COVID stimulus. Years of elevated interest costs appear to be taking their toll on deals struck in a notably different environment than today.

We believe an important yet underrecognized side effect of the increase in BDC redemptions is the trading impact they could have on the syndicated market. Industry research suggests private BDCs typically manage portfolios utilizing a liquidity sleeve of syndicated credit, commonly comprised of bank loans. These allocations vary across managers, with the amount of FASB ASC 820 Level 2 priced assets—an approximation for public credit exposure—ranging from 10% to nearly 40% for some private BDCs. Although it is difficult to estimate a precise level of impact, we believe it's highly likely that redemption-driven technical pressures have contributed to the underperformance of the loan market in recent months, as private BDC managers sell bank loans to meet redemptions. If redemptions continue, this forced selling may create attractive opportunities for active public credit managers to acquire assets below their intrinsic value.

Valuations have improved in credit markets as a result of the market moves seen in Q1. Spreads for the high yield bond and bank loan markets widened 47bps and 59bps, respectively, with index yields climbing to 7.4% for high yield bonds and 8.7% for bank loans. It's worth highlighting that current spread levels for loans remain well above historical medians, while many other areas of fixed income continue to be priced near all-time tights. We believe there are selective opportunities to potentially earn double-digit total returns from senior secured loans through a combination of income and price appreciation.

Portfolio Positioning

From a trading perspective, Q1 was one of our most active in years, as the volatility seen week-to-week created numerous opportunities for us to deploy capital at compelling valuations. During Q1, we acquired assets in the secondary market at an average price of 94.1 for bonds and 92.6 for loans, well below average prices of the respective indices. We purchased bonds and loans from issuers across several sectors, with our largest deployment in software/services and insurance brokerage. Importantly, our portfolio remains focused on our best ideas, with the top 10 issuers representing 31% of the portfolio at quarter-end.

As our longtime investors are aware, we believe the insurance brokerage space is a fundamentally misrated area of credit markets that provides the potential for attractive risk-adjusted returns. The sector underperformed during the quarter amid fears that AI tools could replace brokers in the insurance value chain; we strongly disagree with this view. An AI chatbot may be able to facilitate placement of more commoditized forms of insurance such as auto or home policies, where consumers have been able to competitively price shop online for decades. However, our exposure is largely in brokers who place highly complex and bespoke commercial lines policies and provide corporate risk management services, both of which require high-touch expertise and nuance. We remain confident in the durability of the broker-led advisory model and increased our exposure to the sector overall during the quarter.

Within software, we came into the year light in exposure overall, with less than 4% exposure to the sector at year-end. As a result, we could play "offense" during the quarter and take advantage of the dislocation seen in many software names. Consistent with our philosophy, we were very selective in our purchases, focusing on identifying high-quality, durable assets. To us, durable assets include those that own proprietary data assets (e.g., unique datasets unlikely to be easily replaced), software that is compliance/regulatory driven or with sensitive information (e.g., HR systems), or platforms that have substantial network effects. We doubled our software exposure to 8% of the portfolio at quarter-end, with purchases of debt issued by companies such as Oracle (ORCL), Ultimate Software Group (HR software provider) and Renaissance Learning (educational software provider).

Overall, we believe the portfolio has considerable embedded value, offering the potential for attractive outperformance above market indices going forward. At the end of the quarter, the average price of our portfolio was approximately 93.3 and the portfolio had a yield to worst of 8.4%; the high yield index, by contrast, had an average price of 96.2 and a yield to worst of 7.4%. This embedded value is particularly notable for our loan exposure, where the segment had a yield to worst of 12.3% at quarter-end. As noted previously, we believe select opportunities in loans offer the potential for double-digit total returns if our credit thesis proves correct, which could serve as a potential source of outperformance in the intermediate term.

We acknowledge that our recent performance over the prior quarter and year has lagged market benchmarks. Key pillars of our investment philosophy—such as our emphasis on cross-capital structure investing and a more benchmark-agnostic style regarding sector exposures and ratings—have weighed on shorter term performance when compared to market indices. It's worth noting that our longstanding partners who have entrusted us with their capital have experienced similar periods before, such as our underperformance in 2016 and Q4 2022; these periods were subsequently followed by notable outperformance in 2017 and 2023. Ultimately, we have the utmost confidence that our approach is the optimal way to invest in credit markets over the long term. The embedded value in our portfolio provides the potential for attractive performance that is more consistent with our long-term track record.

Perspective

We believe the shift in market sentiment this year has created a more attractive environment to deploy capital in credit. Geopolitical conflicts, AI-driven disruption fears and private credit concerns have all contributed toward widening credit spreads and rising yields. At the end of the quarter, current yield levels for both the high yield bond and bank loan market offer the potential for high-single-digit returns—often considered "equity like"—with historically less volatility than equity markets.

We firmly believe that periods of increased volatility can create compelling opportunities for active managers, and we have been active year-to-date in acquiring assets at attractive discounts to par. Importantly, we believe that margins of safety should not be compromised in the search for yield, and we continue to maintain our discipline in credit selection, with an unwavering focus on business quality.

Bryan C. Krug, CFA, Portfolio Manager.
